era_context: The French Revolution is consolidating its early reforms: feudalism has been abolished, the Civil Constitution of the Clergy has been enacted, and a constitutional monarchy is being established. The royal family remains in Paris, still nominally cooperating with the Revolution. Across Europe, monarchies are alarmed but not yet openly at war with France. The Russo-Turkish War (1787–1792) is ongoing. In the Americas, the newly independent United States is establishing its federal government under the Constitution. The Industrial Revolution is accelerating in Britain.

events.title: Civil Constitution of the Clergy enacted (France)
events.description: The Civil Constitution of the Clergy, passed by the National Assembly in July 1790 and accepted by King Louis XVI in August, restructured the Catholic Church in France as a department of the state. Bishops and priests were to be elected by local assemblies and paid by the state; the Pope's authority was subordinated to French law. The Constitution required clergy to swear an oath of allegiance to the state. This measure was intended to bring the Church under revolutionary control and eliminate its feudal privileges, but it provoked fierce resistance from the papacy, conservative clergy, and devout Catholics. The refusal of many priests to take the oath created a schism between 'constitutional' and 'refractory' clergy that poisoned French religious life for decades and contributed to the counter-revolutionary movement.
key_figures: Talleyrand (Bishop of Autun, supporter of the Constitution)
Pope Pius VI (opponent of the Constitution)

sources.name: Civil Constitution of the Clergy (primary text)

events.certainty_note: The Civil Constitution is a formal legislative text with precise dates. Its passage and implementation are extensively documented.

events.title: Decree granting civil rights to Sephardic Jews (France)
events.description: On 28 January 1790, the National Assembly granted full civil and political rights to Sephardic Jews in France, particularly those in Bordeaux and Avignon. This decree preceded the more comprehensive emancipation of all French Jews in 1791. The Sephardic community, which had long enjoyed relative tolerance in southern France, was granted citizenship and the right to hold office. This was a significant step toward the universal emancipation of French Jewry and reflected Enlightenment principles of religious toleration and universal rights.
events.certainty_note: The decree is formally documented with precise date. Its passage and content are uncontested.

events.title: Russo-Turkish War: Battle of the Pruth River
events.description: In 1790, Russian forces under General Aleksandr Suvorov achieved a decisive victory over Ottoman forces at the Pruth River, consolidating Russian control of the Danube region. The battle was part of the broader Russo-Turkish War (1787–1792) and demonstrated Russian military superiority. The victory strengthened Russia's negotiating position and contributed to the eventual Russian gains in the Treaty of Jassy (1792). The campaign also saw the capture of several Ottoman fortresses and the devastation of Ottoman territories in the Balkans.

sources.name: Russian military records and Suvorov's correspondence

events.certainty_note: The battle is attested in Russian and Ottoman sources, but exact dates and casualty figures vary. The year 1790 is assigned by convention within the broader war period.

events.title: Nootka Sound Controversy resolved (Spain and Britain)
events.description: The Nootka Sound Controversy, a dispute between Spain and Britain over control of the Pacific Northwest coast, was resolved by the Nootka Sound Convention signed in October 1790. The dispute had arisen from Spanish seizure of British fur trading posts at Nootka Sound in 1789. The convention granted both powers equal rights to trade and settle in the region, effectively ending Spanish monopoly claims on the Pacific coast north of California. The resolution marked a significant shift in imperial power dynamics: Britain's growing naval and commercial strength forced Spain to accept parity in the Pacific, presaging Britain's eventual dominance in the region.
key_figures: William Pitt the Younger (British Prime Minister)
events.certainty_note: The convention is a formal diplomatic agreement with precise date. Its terms and significance are well-documented.

events.title: Synod of Pistoia (Catholic Church reform)
events.description: The Synod of Pistoia, convened by Bishop Scipione de' Ricci in September 1786 but formally concluded in 1790, attempted to reform the Catholic Church along Jansenist and Enlightenment lines. The synod's decrees called for greater lay participation in Church governance, simplified liturgy, and reduced papal authority. The synod was controversial and was condemned by Pope Pius VI in the bull Auctorem Fidei (1794). The Synod of Pistoia represented an attempt to reconcile Catholicism with Enlightenment rationalism and democratic principles, but it was ultimately suppressed and became a symbol of failed reform within the pre-revolutionary Church.
key_figures: Pope Pius VI (opponent of the synod's reforms)
events.certainty_note: The synod's proceedings are documented, though its exact dates and the timeline of its formal conclusion are somewhat unclear in sources. The 1790 date marks its formal conclusion.
cross_references: 1790-001 (Civil Constitution of the Clergy, related reform attempt)

events.title: Haitian Revolution: Mulatto uprising and slave rebellion escalation
events.description: In 1790, the Haitian Revolution intensified as enslaved people and free people of colour (mulattoes) organised coordinated resistance against white colonial rule. The mulatto uprising, led by figures including Vincent Ogé, was brutally suppressed, but the broader slave rebellion continued to spread. By 1790, the rebellion had become a mass movement involving tens of thousands of enslaved people, transforming from isolated resistance into a revolutionary war. The year marked the transition from localised uprisings to a coordinated, island-wide insurgency that would ultimately succeed in overthrowing slavery and colonial rule.
key_figures: Vincent Ogé (mulatto rebel leader, executed in 1791)

sources.name: Later Haitian historiography and oral traditions

events.certainty_note: The broad outline of the 1790 escalation is well-documented in colonial records, but specific dates and casualty figures vary. The year is assigned by convention within the broader revolutionary period.

events.title: U.S. Residence Act and establishment of federal capital
events.description: The Residence Act, signed by President George Washington on 16 July 1790, established the location of the permanent capital of the United States on the Potomac River (present-day Washington, D.C.). The Act was the result of a political compromise between Alexander Hamilton (who wanted a strong federal government and supported assumption of state debts) and Thomas Jefferson and James Madison (who wanted the capital in the South). The compromise, negotiated at a dinner between Hamilton and Jefferson, resolved a major political crisis and established the framework for the federal government's financial system. The capital's location on the Potomac symbolised the balance between North and South in the new republic.

sources.name: Jefferson's account of the dinner compromise

events.certainty_note: The Residence Act is a formal legislative text with precise date. The political compromise and its terms are well-documented.

events.title: Qing Dynasty: Macartney Embassy departs Britain
events.region: Britain and en route to China
events.description: The British Macartney Embassy, led by Lord George Macartney, departed from Britain in September 1792 but preparations and planning occurred throughout 1790–1791. The embassy was dispatched by the British government to establish formal diplomatic relations with the Qing Dynasty and open Chinese ports to British trade. The embassy carried gifts and letters from King George III to the Qianlong Emperor. The journey itself was a major undertaking, involving a voyage around Africa and across the Indian Ocean. The embassy's departure marked Britain's first major diplomatic initiative toward China and reflected growing British commercial ambitions in Asia.
events.certainty_note: The embassy's planning and departure are documented, but the exact date of departure varies slightly in sources (1792 is more commonly cited). The 1790 date marks the beginning of active preparations.

events.title: Tipu Sultan's military modernisation and French alliance
events.description: Tipu Sultan, the ruler of Mysore, continued his efforts to modernise his military and establish alliances against British expansion. In 1790, Tipu sought to strengthen ties with France, which was itself undergoing revolutionary transformation. Tipu was influenced by French military technology and revolutionary ideals, and he attempted to use French support to counter British dominance in southern India. The Third Anglo-Mysore War (1790–1792) was ongoing, with Tipu's forces facing increasing pressure from British and allied Indian armies. Tipu's modernisation efforts, including the introduction of European-style artillery and tactics, made Mysore a formidable military power but ultimately could not overcome British numerical and naval superiority.
events.certainty_note: Tipu's military modernisation and French contacts are well-documented, but specific dates and details of individual initiatives vary between sources.

events.title: Austro-Turkish War: Treaty of Sistova
events.description: The Austro-Turkish War (1788–1791) was concluded by the Treaty of Sistova, signed on 4 August 1791 (but negotiations occurred throughout 1790). The treaty ended Austria's war with the Ottoman Empire, which had been fought alongside Russia. Austria gained some territorial concessions in the Balkans but less than it had hoped. The treaty reflected the declining power of the Ottoman Empire and the rising power of Russia and Austria in the region. The treaty's terms set the stage for further Ottoman territorial losses in the subsequent Russo-Turkish War and the eventual emergence of independent Balkan states in the 19th century.
events.certainty_note: The treaty is a formal diplomatic agreement with precise date. Its terms and significance are well-documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The Civil Constitution of the Clergy is sometimes portrayed as a purely revolutionary measure, but recent scholarship emphasises that it built on earlier Enlightenment critiques of the Church and had some support among reform-minded clergy. The Nootka Sound Convention is sometimes overstated as a complete British victory; Spain retained significant claims and presence in the Pacific Northwest, and the convention was more of a compromise than a decisive shift. The Residence Act's 'dinner compromise' between Hamilton and Jefferson is a famous historical narrative, but some historians question whether the compromise was as neat or as decisive as Jefferson's later account suggests.
historiographic_note: The year 1790 is well-documented for Europe and North America, with rich archival sources for France, Britain, and the United States. Documentation of events in Asia, Africa, and the Ottoman Empire is thinner and often mediated through European sources. The Haitian Revolution is documented primarily through colonial records; enslaved people's own voices are largely absent. The Macartney Embassy's planning is documented in British sources, but Chinese perspectives are less frequently consulted. The year reflects the biases of surviving archives: government records, diplomatic correspondence, and published narratives dominate, while popular movements and non-elite perspectives are underrepresented.

graph_edges.note: The Civil Constitution of the Clergy alienated the Church and contributed to the counter-revolutionary sentiment that motivated the Flight to Varennes.
The 1790 emancipation of Sephardic Jews was followed by the comprehensive emancipation of all French Jews in 1791.
The 1790 escalation of the Haitian Revolution led to the formal coordination at Bois Caïman in 1791.
The Residence Act established the framework for the federal capital, which was occupied in 1800.
